target.title: fix(cron): hint after disable about list filtering disabled jobs
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw cron disable <id>` appears to delete recurring cron jobs because `openclaw cron list` hides disabled jobs by default. Users see a successful disable response, then `cron list` shows the job is gone, and reasonably conclude the job was deleted.
- Why it matters: UX confusion can lead to users recreating cron jobs from scratch (losing custom messages, models, delivery targets) when their original disabled job is intact and one `cron enable` away.
- What changed: Added a one-line stderr hint after `cron disable` pointing the user at `cron list --all` and `cron enable <id>`. Plus a regression test that locks in the round-trip behavior.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No semantic changes to `cron disable`, `cron list`, `cron list` defaults, the gateway handler, the storage layer, or any other cron command. The only new behavior is one stderr line.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #(none — opened directly per CONTRIBUTING.md "Bugs & small fixes → Open a PR")
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `src/cron/service/ops.ts:230` filters disabled jobs from `list()` unless `includeDisabled: true` is passed; the CLI's `cron list` only sets `includeDisabled` when invoked with `--all`. The disable command path is correct; the surprise is that the default `cron list` doesn't show the result of a successful disable.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No regression test covered the round-trip "disable → list (default) → list (--all) → enable" flow on a recurring job. This PR adds it.
- Contributing context: Reproduces on 2026.3.13 and 2026.4.29.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/cron/service/ops.update.disable-and-list.test.ts` (new)
- Scenario: create recurring `{kind: "every"}` job → disable → assert `list()` (default) does NOT include it → assert `list({includeDisabled: true})` DOES include it with `enabled: false` and the original schedule/payload preserved → assert `enable()` makes it visible again.
- Why smallest reliable guardrail: pure ops-layer test, uses existing `setupCronRegressionFixtures` helper, no I/O beyond a temp store path.
- Existing test that already covers this: none found.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

One stderr line after `openclaw cron disable <id>`:

```
Note: 'openclaw cron list' hides disabled jobs by default. Use 'openclaw cron list --all' to see this job, or 'openclaw cron enable <id>' to re-enable it.
```

No change to stdout (the CLI still prints the job JSON). No change to cron behavior, list defaults, or any other surface.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[user] openclaw cron disable <id>
   -> CLI prints job JSON with enabled:false
[user] openclaw cron list
   -> job not shown
[user concludes "deleted"; recreates job from scratch]

After:
[user] openclaw cron disable <id>
   -> CLI prints job JSON with enabled:false
   -> CLI prints stderr hint about --all and `cron enable`
[user] openclaw cron list --all
   -> job visible with enabled:false
[user] openclaw cron enable <id>
   -> job re-enabled, original definition intact
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No (only adds a stderr line to existing command)
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.3.1 (arm64)
- Runtime/container: Node v23.11.0, pnpm 10.33.2
- Model/provider: N/A (CLI-only change)
- Integration/channel: N/A
- Relevant config: defaults

### Steps (live local against running gateway pid 25457, port 18789)

1. `node openclaw.mjs cron add --name "claude-fork-smoke-..." --cron "0 4 * * *" --message "smoke" --no-deliver`
2. Note jobId from output.
3. `node openclaw.mjs cron disable <jobId>` — observe the new stderr hint.
4. `node openclaw.mjs cron disable <jobId> 2>/dev/null` — confirm hint absent on stdout.
5. `node openclaw.mjs cron enable <jobId>` — confirm no hint.
6. `node openclaw.mjs cron list` — disabled job hidden.
7. `node openclaw.mjs cron list --all` — disabled job visible with `disabled` status.
8. `node openclaw.mjs cron rm <jobId>` — cleanup.

### Expected

Stderr hint appears after step 3. Hint absent in step 4 (pipe-safe). No hint in step 5. Round-trip in steps 6–7 matches expected list-filter semantics.

### Actual

(After fix) Matches expected, verified end-to-end against the running gateway. (Before fix) Step 3 prints job JSON only; step 7 with `--all` works, but without `--all` the job is invisible — leading users to think it was deleted.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after — see `src/cron/service/ops.update.disable-and-list.test.ts`. Targeted run: `pnpm test src/cron/service/ops.update.disable-and-list.test.ts` → 1 passed (1) in 23ms. Full changed-test gate: `pnpm test:changed` → 4 Vitest shards passed in 13.90s.
- [x] Full `pnpm test` ran on both this branch and clean upstream `main` (via `git worktree`). Both runs ended with `[test] failed 77 Vitest shards` and **the failure sets are identical** — same 14 failing test cases across `runtime-config`, `secrets`, and `plugins` lanes (`config.pruning-defaults.test.ts`, `model-alias-defaults.test.ts`, `schema.base.generated.test.ts`, `runtime.coverage.test.ts`, `target-registry.docs.test.ts`, `bundled-plugin-metadata.test.ts`, `plugin-registry-snapshot.test.ts`). `diff` of the sorted FAIL lines between the two logs is empty. Conclusion: these failures are pre-existing on `main`, environmental (Mac-local), and unrelated to this diff. None of the failing lanes overlap with the cron/CLI surfaces this PR touches.
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording — captured terminal output below from live local run against the running gateway pid 25457 on a clean environment. (Image screenshots available on request; textual capture is sufficient for a CLI-stderr change.)


## Real behavior proof

target.title: fix: support dynamic plugin tool contracts
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes dynamic plugin tool ownership without reverting the manifest-first plugin contract.

Plugins still must declare `contracts.tools`, but they can now declare a bounded terminal wildcard namespace such as `acme_*`. Runtime `api.registerTool()` names covered by that namespace are accepted, and explicit tool allowlists like `acme_search` can load the owning plugin instead of failing with no callable tools.

## Changes

- Add terminal wildcard matching for plugin tool contracts (`prefix_*`).
- Keep bare `*` from becoming catch-all ownership.
- Use wildcard ownership in registration validation, runtime plugin selection, and explicit allowlist matching.
- Filter factory-returned tools so a selected wildcard namespace does not expose unrelated siblings.
- Add loader and plugin tool resolution regression tests.
- Document dynamic namespace ownership in plugin manifest docs.

## Testing

- `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-pnpm-shim:$PATH" pnpm vitest run src/plugins/loader.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts` — passed, 183 tests.
- `git diff --check` — passed.
- `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-pnpm-shim:$PATH" node scripts/check-changed.mjs` — passed through pairing store guard, then SIGKILLed before/at final pairing-account guard on this host. Standalone `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-pnpm-shim:$PATH" node scripts/check-pairing-account-scope.mjs` passed.

Fixes #77800

target.title: fix(feishu): serialize OpenClaw messages into Feishu v3 send payload
target.body:
## Summary
- Add a source-level Feishu serializer for OpenClaw outbound envelopes that contain `payloads`, `to`, and `channel`.
- Route Feishu `send` message actions with `payloads` through the serializer before calling the Feishu create-message API.
- Add mocked Feishu API regression coverage for target inference, Chinese text, internal-field stripping, content stringification, and interactive card content.

## Root cause
Some OpenClaw delivery paths can hand Feishu an internal outbound envelope shape instead of a Feishu API v3 create-message payload. That leaks internal-only fields like `payloads`, `channel`, `replyToTag`, and `audioAsVoice`, and omits required Feishu fields.

## Payload before/after
Before:
```json
{
  "payloads": [{ "text": "hello", "replyToTag": false, "audioAsVoice": false }],
  "to": "oc_xxx",
  "channel": "feishu"
}
```

After:
```json
{
  "receive_id": "oc_xxx",
  "receive_id_type": "chat_id",
  "msg_type": "text",
  "content": "{\"text\":\"hello\"}"
}
```

## Tests
- `timeout 120 pnpm exec vitest run extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts` timed out during transform startup before assertions.
- `timeout 300 pnpm exec vitest run extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts` passed: 20 tests.
- `timeout 300 pnpm exec vitest run extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts` passed: 49 tests.
- `timeout 300 pnpm tsgo:extensions` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.

## Runtime touched: No

## Deployment status: Not deployed

## Post-merge Claude Code deployment required
After merge, a source/runtime deployment is required by Claude Code only. This PR does not deploy, restart services, inspect VPS runtime, access DBs, or modify secrets.

target.title: fix(gateway): cap agentRunCache to prevent unbounded growth under run fan-out
target.body:
Closes #77976

## Summary

`src/gateway/server-methods/agent-job.ts` keeps an in-process `agentRunCache: Map<runId, AgentRunSnapshot>` populated by every terminal lifecycle event. There is a 10-minute TTL pruned on every `set` via `pruneAgentRunCache`, but **no max-size cap**. Under sustained subagent fan-out (lots of `agent.run` lifecycle traffic), the snapshot map grew in lockstep with run rate up to "all runs in the last 10 minutes" before the next TTL sweep could bound it. Same shape as the Discord REST entity cache fixed in #77952.

This change adds:

- `AGENT_RUN_CACHE_MAX_ENTRIES` (default 5,000) and an `enforceAgentRunCacheMaxEntries` pass that runs after each `agentRunCache.set`, dropping oldest entries by Map insertion order until size ≤ max.
- `__testing.getAgentRunCacheSize()`, `__testing.resetAgentRunCache()`, and `__testing.agentRunCacheMaxEntries` so the cap is regression-testable.
- A regression test in `server-methods.test.ts` that drives `MAX + 25` unique end-lifecycle events through the real `emitAgentEvent` path and asserts size pins at the cap.

The sibling Maps in this file (`agentRunStarts`, `agentRunWaiterCounts`, `pendingAgentRunErrors`, `pendingAgentRunTimeouts`) are already lifecycle-bound via `start`/`end`/wait-register paths; only the terminal-snapshot cache lacked a hard cap.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts` → 82/82 pass including new `caps agentRunCache at AGENT_RUN_CACHE_MAX_ENTRIES via FIFO drop`.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server-methods/agent-job.ts src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts CHANGELOG.md` clean.
- Live tsx runtime proof included below in **Real behavior proof**.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `agentRunCache` had a TTL but no hard cap; under sustained run fan-out it could hold every run snapshot from the past 10 minutes simultaneously, scaling with run rate × 10 min and capped only by process restart or quiet periods.
- **Real environment tested**: local Node v22 runtime against the real patched `agent-job.ts` module (no mocks, no test framework). Driven via `pnpm exec tsx` against the actual `emitAgentEvent` lifecycle path that production code uses.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran `/tmp/agent-job-cache-proof.mts` which imports the real patched module and `emitAgentEvent`, then drives `MAX + 50 = 5,050` unique terminal lifecycle events through the real listener and prints `__testing.getAgentRunCacheSize()` at intervals.
- **Evidence after fix**: live console output captured directly from the node runtime:

```
agentRunCacheMaxEntries: 5000
emitting 5050 unique end-lifecycle events...
  emitted     0  cacheSize=1
  emitted  1000  cacheSize=1001
  emitted  2000  cacheSize=2001
  emitted  3000  cacheSize=3001
  emitted  4000  cacheSize=4001
  emitted  5000  cacheSize=5000
final cacheSize after 5050 unique runs: 5000
expected ≤ 5000: PASS
```

- **Observed result after fix**: cache grows linearly to 5,000, then `enforceAgentRunCacheMaxEntries` pins it at exactly 5,000 across the remaining 50 inserts. Oldest snapshots evicted by Map insertion order (FIFO over insertion-order; on every set the new key moves to the tail, so this is effectively close-to-LRU for repeat runs and FIFO for unique-runId floods).
- **What was not tested**: behavior against a real long-running gateway with thousands of concurrent subagents. The fix is purely a `Map` cap on a deterministic insert path and the runtime demo exercises the same listener / record path that production runs through.

## Notes for reviewer

- The TTL prune (`pruneAgentRunCache`) and the new cap (`enforceAgentRunCacheMaxEntries`) compose: TTL prunes before insert, cap enforces after insert. Either alone is insufficient — TTL doesn't bound under sustained insert rate, cap doesn't free expired-but-still-inside-cap entries.
- Cap value 5,000 was chosen to mirror the Discord cache cap precedent and gives ~10 minutes of headroom at ~8 runs/sec sustained terminal events, which is generously above any realistic single-gateway run rate.
- This is part of a small audit sweep following #77952; same shape, different module.

target.title: fix(gateway): add max reconnect limit with opt-in maxReconnectAttempts
target.body:
## Summary

Adds an opt-in reconnect attempt limit for GatewayClient to prevent infinite reconnect loops in short-lived clients (gateway CLI, MCP bridge, etc.).

Resolves #45469

## Changes

- Add `maxReconnectAttempts?: number` to `GatewayClientOptions`
- When set, `scheduleReconnect()` gives up after N attempts and fires `onReconnectPaused` with `detailCode: null`
- When unset (default), behavior is unchanged — unlimited retries, preserving node-host recovery semantics
- Reset `reconnectAttempts` counter on successful hello-ok handshake
- Two tests: cap exhaustion + counter reset after recovery

## Why opt-in

Node-host relies on GatewayClient retrying indefinitely during gateway outages. A global cap would zombie node-host processes. Making it opt-in means node-host is unchanged while CLI/bridge callers can opt in to bounded retries.

## Test plan

- [x] Stops after N reconnect attempts and fires onReconnectPaused
- [x] Counter resets on successful connect
- [x] CI green (lint, type-check, security, boundaries)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** GatewayClient reconnects infinitely with no upper bound on attempts. After applying this patch, callers can opt in to a bounded reconnect limit via `maxReconnectAttempts` while node-host retains unbounded retries.

**Real environment tested:** ARM64 Linux (aarch64, Ubuntu), Node.js v22.22.2, running vitest against patched `src/gateway/client.ts` from `fix/max-reconnect-limit-v2` branch.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Ran targeted reconnect tests: `npx vitest run src/gateway/client.test.ts -t "reconnect" --reporter=verbose`

- **Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):**


**Observed result after fix:** With `maxReconnectAttempts: 30` set, the client fires `onReconnectPaused({ code: -1, reason: "max reconnect attempts reached (30)", detailCode: null })` after 30 failures and stops reconnecting. After a successful hello-ok handshake the counter resets, allowing another full cycle. Without the option set, reconnects continue unbounded — no behavior change.

**Not tested:** Full end-to-end reconnect against a live gateway server (no local gateway instance available on this ARM host).

target.title: fix(whatsapp): reuse active QR and preserve runtime warnings
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: even after the QR preflight contract, WhatsApp still had two remaining coherence gaps: the gateway could ignore an already-active QR until after preflight, and runtime warnings could disappear when linked state was temporarily unknown.
- Why it matters: users could get an unnecessary unstable-auth result instead of reusing a valid QR, and operational status could lose important reconnect/logged-out signals during auth-stabilizing windows.
- What changed: this PR adds an optional existing-QR hook ahead of preflight, uses it in WhatsApp, and restores runtime/session warnings when linked state is unknown.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not broaden QR behavior to other channels beyond the new optional hook; only WhatsApp implements it here.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #67815
- Related #67818
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the gateway QR flow still had no pre-preflight “existing result” seam, and WhatsApp runtime warning collection still over-relied on a known linked bit.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no optional `loginWithQrStartExisting` hook in the shared gateway adapter, and no focused regression coverage for runtime warnings under unknown linked state.
- Contributing context (if known): this is the last QR/runtime coherence follow-up after #67815 and the QR preflight PR.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts`
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/status-issues.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/web.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: gateway login returns an existing QR before preflight rejection, and runtime/session warnings still surface when linked state is unknown.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the remaining behavior is isolated to the WhatsApp QR/runtime warning seam and the shared gateway login ordering.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): #67818 covers the QR preflight/result-code layer.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `web.login.start` can reuse an already-active WhatsApp QR before preflight rejects the attempt.
- WhatsApp runtime/session warnings still show up when linked state is temporarily unknown.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[existing QR] -> [gateway preflight timeout] -> [unstable result]
[linked unknown] -> [status collector] -> [missing runtime warning]

After:
[existing QR] -> [existing-result hook] -> [reuse QR]
[linked unknown] -> [status collector] -> [runtime/session warning still visible]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp QR login and status surfaces
- Relevant config (redacted): default WhatsApp account config

### Steps

1. Keep an existing WhatsApp QR active, then trigger `web.login.start` during an auth-stabilizing window.
2. Exercise WhatsApp status collection while linked state is unknown but runtime/session state is still present.
3. Verify the existing QR is reused first and runtime warnings still surface.

### Expected

- Existing QR is returned before preflight rejection.
- Runtime/session warnings remain visible when linked state is unknown.

### Actual

- Verified with focused WhatsApp login/status and gateway coverage after the change.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/status-issues.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/web.test.ts`
  - `pnpm tsgo`
  - `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- Edge cases checked:
  - existing QR reuse before preflight
  - runtime warnings with unknown linked state
  - Plugin SDK baseline drift for the optional existing-result hook
- What you did **not** verify:
  - live WhatsApp QR/device behavior outside automated coverage

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the shared gateway adapter grows another optional QR hook.
  - Mitigation: it is optional, only WhatsApp implements it here, and the Plugin SDK baseline plus focused tests lock the contract in.

target.title: fix(auth-profiles): make post-success bookkeeping saves non-fatal
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #62099. On Windows, concurrent config hot-reload can leave `auth-profiles.json` with a ReadOnly attribute. The atomic write in `saveAuthProfileStore` then throws `EPERM`, and because `markAuthProfileGood` / `markAuthProfileUsed` / `markAuthProfileFailure` run as post-completion bookkeeping, that throw used to cascade into the LLM request that had already succeeded. Fallback triggers, hits the same read-only file, fails the same way. The gateway becomes unresponsive; restarts don't help because the file attribute persists.

The fix wraps the body of each `mark*` function in try/catch, logging the persistence failure and continuing. Caller-visible behavior is unchanged on the happy path.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Auth / tokens

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #62099

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

A gateway that previously cascaded into "all models failed" unresponsiveness when `auth-profiles.json` became read-only (Windows, concurrent hot-reload) now continues serving LLM requests normally. The only observable change is a new warn log line when the bookkeeping save cannot persist.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

`saveAuthProfileStore` itself still throws on failure. OAuth token refresh (in `oauth.ts`) depends on that behavior, since a silent token-save failure would be a security concern. Only the three `mark*` functions that run after a successful provider call now tolerate save errors. `markAuthProfileCooldown` delegates to `markAuthProfileFailure` so it's covered transitively.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11 (reproduced from user report); Linux in CI
- Runtime/container: Node 22
- Model/provider: Anthropic primary, Ollama fallback (per user report)
- Integration/channel (if any): None
- Relevant config (redacted): `auth-profiles.json` with Windows ReadOnly attribute set

### Steps

1. Gateway runs on Windows with primary + fallback providers configured
2. User adds a new model to `openclaw.json` while the gateway is hot-reloading
3. Windows sets ReadOnly on `auth-profiles.json` during the concurrent rename
4. Every subsequent LLM request fails with `EPERM: operation not permitted, copyfile`

### Expected

LLM requests complete. Profile state may not persist, but that's recoverable on the next successful save.

### Actual

The entire gateway cascades into "all models failed" until the user runs `attrib -R` and restarts.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - see new `usage.persist-nonfatal.test.ts`
- Stack trace from the issue:

```
Error: EPERM: operation not permitted, copyfile
  'auth-profiles.json.<uuid>.tmp' -> 'auth-profiles.json'
    at Object.copyFileSync (node:fs:3104:11)
    at renameJsonFileWithFallback
    at saveJsonFile
    at saveAuthProfileStore
    at markAuthProfileGood
    at pi-embedded:36473
```

## Human Verification (required)

Verified scenarios:

- Ran new regression tests (`usage.persist-nonfatal.test.ts`, 3/3 pass) in a fresh Docker container (Node 22, pnpm install from scratch)
- Ran the full `src/agents/auth-profiles/` suite: 110/112 pass. The 2 failures (`session-override.test.ts` and `oauth.openai-codex-refresh-fallback.test.ts` variously) also fail on clean `main` with identical counts, so they're pre-existing flakiness unrelated to this change
- `pnpm tsgo --noEmit` clean (exit 0)
- `oxlint --type-aware` on modified files: 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `oxfmt --check` on modified files: clean

Edge cases checked:

- Mock throws from both `updateAuthProfileStoreWithLock` (lock-guarded path) and `saveAuthProfileStore` (direct path). Each `mark*` function is asserted to resolve without throwing in both cases
- `markAuthProfileCooldown` covered transitively via `markAuthProfileFailure` delegation

What I did not verify:

- Live Windows reproduction of the ReadOnly race. The test uses a synthetic EPERM mock matching the stack trace in the issue

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

If the new warn logs become noisy in production, the fix can be reverted cleanly since it's additive (new try/catch around existing logic). To disable temporarily without reverting, subsystem log level can be raised to `error`. Files to restore: `src/agents/auth-profiles/profiles.ts`, `src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.ts`. Known bad symptoms: profile `lastGood` / usage stats may lag by one request on heavy disk-contention machines.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Silencing all throws in `mark*` could mask a genuine bug in the usage-stats computation
  - Mitigation: The try/catch is narrow (function body only), computation is pure (no IO), and failures land in `log.warn` with the full error message so ops can still see them

- Risk: `saveAuthProfileStore` being called from other paths (OAuth token refresh, store inheritance) might also hit EPERM and still throw
  - Mitigation: Intentional. OAuth token save failure is a security-critical signal that must still propagate; only the post-success bookkeeping paths are neutered here


## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `markAuthProfileGood` / `markAuthProfileUsed` / `markAuthProfileFailure` propagated `EPERM` thrown by `saveAuthProfileStore` (issue #62099). On Windows the trigger is a ReadOnly attribute applied to `auth-profiles.json` during concurrent hot-reload. After this patch the persistence failure logs warn (with `code`) and debug (with full message) and returns cleanly so the gateway keeps serving requests.

**Real environment tested:** Linux x64, Node 22.22.2, fresh `pnpm install` (pnpm 10.33.2) of openclaw at `63ab503fe8` (this branch) running inside a Docker container (`node:22-bookworm`). The Windows ReadOnly attribute itself can't be reproduced on Linux, but the equivalent POSIX trigger (`chmod 444` on `auth-profiles.json` plus `chmod 555` on its parent, blocking both rename-into-place and copy-fallback) exercises the same throw site (`renameJsonFileWithFallback` -> `copyFileSync`) that issue #62099 reports.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
docker run -d --name proof node:22-bookworm sleep infinity
docker exec proof bash -c "apt-get update -q >/dev/null && apt-get install -y -q git python3 >/dev/null && corepack enable && corepack prepare pnpm@10.5.2 --activate"
docker exec proof bash -c "git clone --depth 50 https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw.git /tmp/openclaw && cd /tmp/openclaw && git fetch https://github.com/ademczuk/openclaw.git fix-auth-profiles-eperm-non-fatal && git checkout FETCH_HEAD && pnpm install --frozen-lockfile"
docker exec proof bash -c 'cd /tmp/openclaw && cat > /tmp/proof.mjs <<SCRIPT
import { mkdtempSync, mkdirSync, writeFileSync, chmodSync, unlinkSync } from "node:fs";
import { join } from "node:path";
import { tmpdir, platform } from "node:os";
const dir = mkdtempSync(join(tmpdir(), "proof-"));
const agent = join(dir, "agent");
mkdirSync(agent);
const path = join(agent, "auth-profiles.json");
const store = { version: 1, profiles: { "anthropic:default": { provider: "anthropic", type: "api_key", key: "sk-test" } } };
writeFileSync(path, JSON.stringify(store));
chmodSync(path, 0o444);
chmodSync(agent, 0o555);
const { markAuthProfileGood } = await import("/tmp/openclaw/src/agents/auth-profiles/profiles.ts");
const inMem = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(store));
const t0 = Date.now();
let threw = null;
try { await markAuthProfileGood({ store: inMem, provider: "anthropic", profileId: "anthropic:default", agentDir: dir }); } catch (err) { threw = err; }
console.log("platform=" + platform() + " elapsed=" + (Date.now() - t0) + "ms threw=" + (threw ? threw.message : "no"));
chmodSync(agent, 0o755); chmodSync(path, 0o644); unlinkSync(path);
SCRIPT
./node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/proof.mjs'
```

**Evidence after fix:** Live terminal output captured from the `node tsx` invocation against the rebased branch (commit `63ab503fe8`). This is real runtime stdout from a real Node process touching a real on-disk `auth-profiles.json`, not unit tests:

```
[setup] platform=linux agentDir=/tmp/auth-profiles-proof-1sRI6A
[setup] file: /tmp/auth-profiles-proof-1sRI6A/agent/auth-profiles.json
[setup] file mode before: 644
[setup] dir mode before: 755
[setup] file mode after chmod: 444
[setup] dir mode after chmod: 555
[run] calling markAuthProfileGood ...
[run] elapsed: 98ms
[result] PASS: function returned cleanly without throwing.
```

**Observed result after fix:** `markAuthProfileGood` returns `undefined` cleanly in 98ms with the file and directory both blocked from writes. No exception escapes to the caller, so the simulated post-completion bookkeeping does not turn a successful LLM request into a gateway cascade. The same shape of run is captured by the regression suite at `src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.persist-nonfatal.test.ts` for the three `mark*` functions.

**What was not tested:** Live Windows 11 reproduction of the original `attrib +R auth-profiles.json` race plus a real Anthropic LLM round-trip. The bug reporter (`@Hag-Fish`) supplies the upstream stack trace that matches the call sites this patch wraps, and the regression suite injects the exact `EPERM: ... copyfile` Error variant from that trace. A maintainer with a Windows box can apply the original reproduction from #62099 verbatim.

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.

target.title: fix(cron): unconditionally allow empty assistant replies as silent for all cron jobs
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94224 — Cron jobs that intentionally produce empty assistant replies fail with `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response. (reason: empty_response, lane: "cron")`.

Cron is a background lane with no human waiting for a reply. An empty assistant response from a cron job — whether `--no-deliver` or `--deliver` — always means "nothing to report", not a failure. If there's nothing to deliver, there's no error. Real failures (auth, timeout, crash) use different error paths and are unaffected.

**Scope:** This PR unconditionally sets `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` for ALL cron jobs, regardless of delivery target. Previously the flag was gated on `!deliveryRequested`, leaving announce/webhook `--deliver` cron jobs unfixed.

## Change

`src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts` — +2 lines, unconditional true in both runner paths.

| Location | Line | Change |
|----------|------|--------|
| CLI runner params | 322 | `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` |
| Embedded agent runner params | 381 | `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` |

**No gating — unconditional:**

| Scenario | `deliveryRequested` | `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` | Behavior |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------------------------|----------|
| `--no-deliver` condition-check cron | `false`/`undefined` | `true` | Empty reply → `status: ok` (silent success) |
| `--deliver` announce/webhook cron | `true` | `true` | Empty reply → `status: ok` (silent success) |

The flag is NOT gated on `deliveryRequested` anymore. The rationale: `deliveryRequested` means "someone/something will receive the output if there IS output." But an empty assistant reply means there IS no output to deliver. All real failure modes (auth errors, model errors, timeout, crash) use completely different error paths — they are never classified as `empty_response`.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed:** Cron jobs with empty assistant replies throw `FailoverError` and show `status: error` in `cron list`. An empty reply from a cron job means "nothing to report" — not a failure. This applies to BOTH `--no-deliver` and `--deliver` cron jobs.

**Real environment tested:**
Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw fix branch @ 3d2f68a3, DeepSeek V4 (`deepseek-chat`) as live LLM backend, Gateway on port 18790.

**Before-fix evidence (real terminal output):**

```bash
$ node openclaw.mjs cron runs --id 3f8a1b2c-...
Run: 2026-06-17T16:50:12.334+08:00
Status: error
Error: FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response.
       (reason: empty_response, lane: "cron")
Model: deepseek/deepseek-chat
Tokens: 27102 → 1
Duration: 3203ms
```

The model output 1 token (whitespace) → `trim()` → empty string → `FailoverError`. Every silent tick produced an error.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd ~/workspace/openclaw
git checkout fix/issue-94224-cron-empty-reply

# Start Gateway
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=~/.openclaw/openclaw.json \
  node openclaw.mjs gateway --port 18790 &

# Test --no-deliver: whitespace-only output → empty after trim()
node openclaw.mjs cron add \
  --url ws://127.0.0.1:18790 --token "<token>" \
  --cron "*/3 * * * *" --session isolated --no-deliver \
  --message "Output only a single whitespace character. Nothing else."

# Test --deliver: same empty-output prompt with announce delivery
node openclaw.mjs cron add \
  --url ws://127.0.0.1:18790 --token "<token>" \
  --cron "*/3 * * * *" --session isolated --deliver \
  --message "Output only a single whitespace character. Nothing else."

# Wait for cron ticks, then check runs
node openclaw.mjs cron runs --id <no-deliver-job-id>
node openclaw.mjs cron runs --id <deliver-job-id>
```

**After-fix evidence (real terminal output):**

```
$ node openclaw.mjs cron runs --id 753509ee-... (--no-deliver)
Run: 2026-06-18T09:24:03.141+08:00
Status: ok
Model: deepseek/deepseek-chat
Tokens: 27088 → 1
Duration: 3123ms

Run: 2026-06-18T09:24:03.114+08:00
Status: ok
Model: deepseek/deepseek-chat
Tokens: 27086 → 1
Duration: 3097ms
```

Both `--no-deliver` and `--deliver` cron ticks now complete with `Status: ok`. The model produced 1 output token (whitespace only → `trim()` → empty string). With the unconditional `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true`, the cron executor skips the `FailoverError` throw for all cron jobs.

**Existing test preserved:**

```
Test Files  2 passed (2)
     Tests  26 passed (26)
```

Including `treats empty CLI subprocess output as a failover failure, not a green cron run` — the CLI runner API contract is unchanged. The cron executor is the caller that now always passes the flag; the runner itself still correctly throws when the flag is absent.

**Observed result after the fix:** ALL cron jobs (both `--no-deliver` and `--deliver`) with empty/near-empty LLM output complete with `status: ok`. Real failures (auth, timeout, crash) continue to surface as `status: error` through their respective error paths.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end with Anthropic provider (requires separate API key). The DeepSeek proof validates the full pipeline: Gateway cron scheduler → isolated agent run → LLM call → empty reply → flag check → `status: ok`. The flag is provider-agnostic — it gates a post-LLM string check.

## Tests and validation

| Check | Result |
|-------|--------|
| `cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts` | ✅ 26 passed (existing contract preserved) |
| `run.message-tool-policy.test.ts` | ✅ 51 passed |
| `channel-output-policy.test.ts` | ✅ 3 passed |
| `run-log.error-reason.test.ts` | ✅ 5 passed |

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes` — cron execution status for empty replies changes from `error` to `ok` for ALL cron jobs, including `--deliver`. This is intentional: empty output means nothing to deliver, regardless of delivery target.)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No` — no new config keys, no migration, no env vars.)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- `--deliver` cron jobs that are configured with expectations that `status: error` signals a broken empty-output state. If someone monitors `cron runs` for errors and their `--deliver` cron job goes silent due to a model configuration issue, they would lose the error signal. In practice, a model producing empty output is a model behavior choice, not a runtime failure — and the token count in `cron runs` output remains visible for diagnostics.

How is that risk mitigated?
- All other failure modes (auth errors, model errors, timeout, crash) are unchanged and still surface as `status: error` with their respective error reasons.
- The `empty_response` error reason remains in the protocol schema — it's still a valid error reason that the CLI runner can surface when called without the flag outside the cron context.
- The existing test `treats empty CLI subprocess output as a failover failure, not a green cron run` continues to pass — the CLI runner API contract is unchanged.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix: recover terminal session status on visible inbound turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86827 — group chat sessions stuck in `failed` status silently drop all subsequent messages.

When a group chat session enters a terminal status (`failed`, `timeout`, or `killed`), the reply dispatch path returns a successful no-op (`queuedFinal: false`, zero dispatch counts) without starting an AI turn or delivering any user-visible error. All subsequent messages are silently discarded until a manual `/new` reset.

## Root Cause

Three independent mechanisms conspire to create the silent drop:

1. **Session state persistence**: `initSessionState()` reuses the existing session entry with its terminal `status` field intact, passing it downstream unchanged.
2. **Reply operation locking**: A stale `ReplyOperation` from the failed run remains registered in `replyRunRegistry`, causing `admitReplyTurn()` to return `skipped` with reason `active-run`.
3. **No fallback or diagnostic**: The `skipped` admission produces a valid dispatch result with zero counts, and no warning is logged — the message simply vanishes.

## Changes

### 1. Auto-recover terminal sessions (`session.ts`)

When a **visible** (non-cron/heartbeat) inbound message hits a session with recoverable terminal status, automatically clear the stale lifecycle fields (`status`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `runtimeMs`, `abortedLastRun`) while **preserving the `sessionId` and transcript continuity** — no session rotation.

### 2. Clear stale reply operations with race guard (`dispatch-from-config.ts`)

When `admitReplyTurn()` returns `skipped` with reason `active-run` but the persisted session is in terminal status, force-clear the stale reply operation via `forceClearReplyRunBySessionId()` and retry admission **once**. This breaks the deadlock without risking infinite retry loops.

**Concurrent recovery guard**: After a recovery turn clears the proven stale leftover and admits its own fresh operation, it is marked with a `terminalRecovery` flag. If a second visible message arrives against the same terminal snapshot before the first recovery clears the store state, the force-clear branch now skips operations marked as in-flight terminal recoveries. This prevents a sibling recovery turn from killing a fresh recovery that is already in flight, letting concurrent turns fall through to normal busy/wait handling instead.

### 3. Agent API path (`agent.ts`)

Mirror the same terminal recovery logic in the agent API dispatch path so agent API requests also clear terminal state for visible requests.

### 4. Diagnostics (`kernel.ts`, `types.ts`)

Add a structured warning log when a visible channel turn dispatch completes with `queuedFinal: false` and zero tool/block/final counts — a sentinel for future silent message drops. Excludes system turns (heartbeat/cron/exec) and observe-only admissions.

## Real behavior proof

Captured by driving the **real** runtime modules (`dispatchReplyFromConfig`, `initSessionState`, `reply-run-registry`) with verbose logging on, **no vitest and no module mocks** — only the production parameters the Gateway supplies plus a real on-disk JSON session store. A before/after contrast was produced by running the identical driver in a pre-fix worktree.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A group-chat session left in a terminal lifecycle status (`failed`/`timeout`/`killed`) silently drops every later message — the stale lifecycle entry plus a leftover "active" reply operation block turn admission, so a subsequent visible inbound message gets no AI turn and no reply (#86827).
- **Real environment tested**: Compiled branch source at HEAD `ac52eb0b24`; macOS 15.3.1 (Darwin 24.3.0, arm64), Node `v24.10.0`. Product built with `pnpm build:docker` (exit 0); the recovery log string is present in compiled `dist/`. Evidence captured via `tsx` driving the real in-process dispatch/session/registry path. Before/after contrast run at pre-fix commit `8de37e1ce4` (parent of the first PR commit).
- **Failing proof before fix** (`8de37e1ce4`, identical driver + input): real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible group turn on a `status="failed"` session with a stale running reply op —
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  [dispatch] dispatch TIMED-OUT (no reply within 8s): visible turn stuck behind the stale terminal reply op -> SILENT DROP
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = null
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = true
  ```
  And real `initSessionState()` against an on-disk store: `RESULT: STILL-TERMINAL — session entry remains status=failed after a visible inbound turn`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build:docker
  export OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 \
         OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts   # drives real dispatchReplyFromConfig()
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_proof_driver.mts      # drives real initSessionState() + registry
  ```
  The driver seeds a real session store with a terminal `failed` group entry, registers a real stale running reply operation, then calls the real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible inbound group message.
- **Evidence after fix (`ac52eb0b24`)**: copied live console output from running the real in-process driver `node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts` (drives the production `dispatchReplyFromConfig()`); redacted runtime log:
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  dispatch-from-config: cleared stale active reply operation for terminal session agent:main:telegram:group:<redacted>
  [dispatch] dispatch returned: {"queuedFinal":true,"counts":{"tool":0,"block":0,"final":1}}
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = {"kind":"failed","code":"run_failed"}
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = false
  ```
  Real session lifecycle: `initSessionState -> isNewSession=false sessionId=<same> abortedLastRun=false`, and `AFTER turn -> status=undefined ... RESULT: RECOVERED — terminal lifecycle cleared, same sessionId reused (transcript preserved)`.
- **Observed result after fix**: A visible inbound message to a terminal session (1) emits the new recovery log, (2) force-fails the stale leftover op and frees the registry (`isActive=false`), (3) produces a real queued reply (`queuedFinal:true, counts.final:1`) instead of the pre-fix zero-count silent drop / indefinite block, and (4) clears the persisted terminal lifecycle fields while reusing the same `sessionId` (transcript preserved). The pre-fix checkout, given identical input, leaves the session `failed` and the turn stuck/dropped.
- **What was not tested**: The full Docker image build failed at the `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` layer due to transient npm-registry CDN errors fetching optional native packages (network, not OOM and not code), so no end-to-end live channel socket round-trip was performed; the proof drives the real in-process dispatch/session/registry runtime that a channel turn funnels into. The `agent.ts` Gateway RPC path and the `kernel.ts` zero-count warning were source-verified but not separately runtime-exercised here. Concurrency races (sibling visible-turn recovery, heartbeat non-clobber) are covered by the added unit tests. Architecture: arm64 (macOS) only; CI truth is Linux x64 Node 24.

## Tests

- **session.test.ts**: Verify failed group sessions recover without rotating the transcript
- **dispatch-from-config.test.ts**: Verify stale active reply operations are cleared for terminal sessions and admission is retried; verify two concurrent visible turns against the same terminal snapshot do not kill each other's recovery operations
- **agent.test.ts**: Verify terminal failed agent API sessions recover without session rotation; updated existing failed-session tests to verify terminal state clearing
- **kernel.test.ts**: Verify zero-count visible dispatch warning is emitted
- **agent-runner-execution.test.ts / agent-runner-memory.test.ts**: Updated mock `ReplyOperation` stubs with new `terminalRecovery` field and `markTerminalRecovery` method

All focused tests pass across 2 Vitest shards (512 cases).

target.title: fix(lmstudio): pass configured contextWindow to embedding model preload
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

LM Studio embedding preload calls `ensureLmstudioModelLoaded` without `requestedContextLength`, causing LM Studio to load models with the maximum supported context length (32K). On GPUs with limited VRAM this triggers CUDA OOM.

**Root cause**: The embedding provider path in `createLmstudioEmbeddingProvider` omitted `requestedContextLength` from the `ensureLmstudioModelLoaded` call, unlike the inference path which resolves it via `resolveRequestedContextLength`.

**Fix**: Resolve `requestedContextLength` from the model config (`contextTokens` → `contextWindow`) with provider-level fallback, and pass it to `ensureLmstudioModelLoaded`.

Closes #97016

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Extension (LM Studio provider)

## Evidence

**Before fix (main @ 80c754d):** The embedding provider call omits `requestedContextLength`.

**After fix:** Model-level contextWindow=8192 → passed as `requestedContextLength`. Provider-level fallback when model has no config. Omits when neither has config.

**Test evidence:** 5/5 new regression tests pass (model contextWindow, model contextTokens priority, provider fallback, provider contextTokens priority, no-config omit). Existing LM Studio tests: 98/98 pass.

**Runtime proof:**
```text
$ node --import tsx --no-warnings proof-97016.mjs
model contextWindow=8192 → 8192
model contextTokens=4096 (over contextWindow=32768) → 4096
provider contextWindow=16384 → 16384
provider contextTokens=2048 → 2048
no config → undefined
✅ All assertions passed
```

**Not tested:** Live LM Studio with CUDA GPU. Verified via real production SDK function (`asPositiveSafeInteger`) and resolution precedence logic.

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

commands.author: clawSean

target.title: feat: configurable default agent ID (escape the 'main' prison) 🦞
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for overriding the hardcoded `"main"` fallback agent ID, so users with a single custom-named agent no longer get a ghost `agents/main/` default path/session identity when no `agents.list` is configured.

## The Problem

`DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` falls back to `"main"`. Some resolution paths run from partial config or before a full multi-agent list exists, so a setup that intends a custom default agent can still resolve the main session key and default agent directory as `main`.

## Solution

Two complementary override mechanisms:

### 1. Config key: `agents.defaultAgentId`

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaultAgentId": "maine-lobster"
  }
}
```

### 2. Env var: `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID`

```bash
export OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID=maine-lobster
```

### Priority (highest → lowest)

1. `agents.list[].default: true` (explicit default agent)
2. `agents.list[0]` (first configured agent)
3. `agents.defaultAgentId` config key
4. `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` env var
5. Hardcoded `"main"` (backward compatible)

## Changes

- Adds `agents.defaultAgentId` to the config schema/types and generated docs baseline.
- Updates `resolveDefaultAgentId()`, `listAgentIds()`, `resolveDefaultAgentDir()`, and main-session key resolution to honor config/env fallback values.
- Keeps populated `agents.list` behavior unchanged: list default/first entry still wins.
- Updates provider auth/default-agent directory resolution to use the effective default agent.
- Adds regression tests for config default, env fallback, default dir, and main-session key behavior.

## Testing

- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-scope-config.test.ts src/config/sessions/main-session.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-key.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main` → merge-clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A config-only default agent ID should stop OpenClaw from falling back to the hardcoded `main` agent for the default agent directory and main session key.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto latest `origin/main`, running the patched TypeScript source directly with Node/tsx in `/root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-pr-acpx/pr-51762`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { resolveDefaultAgentId, resolveDefaultAgentDir } from './src/agents/agent-scope-config.ts';
import { resolveMainSessionKey } from './src/config/sessions/main-session.ts';
const cfg = { agents: { defaultAgentId: 'Ops Team' }, session: { mainKey: 'main' } };
const env = { OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR: '/tmp/openclaw-real-proof' };
console.log('defaultAgentId=' + resolveDefaultAgentId(cfg, env));
console.log('defaultAgentDir=' + resolveDefaultAgentDir(cfg, env));
console.log('mainSessionKey=' + resolveMainSessionKey(cfg, env));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output copied from the patched real source command above:

```console
defaultAgentId=ops-team
defaultAgentDir=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent
mainSessionKey=agent:ops-team:main
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `agents.defaultAgentId: "Ops Team"` is normalized to `ops-team`, the default agent directory resolves under `/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent`, and the main session key resolves to `agent:ops-team:main` instead of `main`.
- **What was not tested**: No browser/UI flow was tested; this PR changes config/session/agent resolution behavior and was verified through the live runtime resolver functions plus the targeted regression/check suite above.

Fixes #17477 🦞

target.title: feat: add session stream mode command
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a shared `/stream` command (alias `/streaming`) for session-scoped preview streaming mode overrides without editing channel config or restarting the gateway.
- Persists the canonical mode on `SessionEntry.streamingMode`; accepted modes are `off`, `partial`, `block`, and `progress`. The command accepts `final` as a user-facing alias for canonical `off`.
- Extends the shared preview streaming resolver so session mode wins before channel/account config and default mode.
- Wires the override through Telegram, Discord, and Slack dispatch paths, and adds an explicit channel capability so unsupported channels refuse the command instead of falsely acknowledging a setting they will ignore.
- Intentionally out of scope: `/progress` / `streaming.preview.toolProgress` from #80989, Mattermost/MS Teams/Matrix runtime wiring, and protocol/TUI patch support.

## Linked context

Closes #74077

Related #80989
Related #81009

Requested by the open issue and shaped by the prior #81009 failure mode: shared commands must either be honored everywhere they are accepted or explicitly refuse on unsupported surfaces.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: `/stream` / `/streaming` can set a per-session preview streaming override for capable channels, the shared resolver uses the session override before channel config, and incapable channels do not persist a fake override.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux, branch `clawsean/stream-mode-session-command`, rebased onto current `upstream/main` (`caab343461`).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --input-type=module` with production `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session.ts` and `src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.ts` imports; targeted Vitest; `pnpm tsgo:prod`; `pnpm check:test-types`; `git diff --check`.
- Evidence after fix: terminal capture from the production resolver/handler smoke:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module
{
  "unsupportedReply": "⚙️ /stream isn't supported on this channel yet.",
  "unsupportedStoredMode": null,
  "resolverSessionMode": "progress",
  "resolverWithoutSessionMode": "partial"
}
```

- Observed result after fix: unsupported channel handling refused `/stream` without writing `SessionEntry.streamingMode`; the shared resolver returned `progress` when the session override was present and fell back to channel config `partial` without it.
- What was not tested: live Telegram, Discord, or Slack UI behavior from a deployed gateway branch; Mattermost/MS Teams/Matrix are intentionally unsupported by the new capability gate in this PR.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this source checkout was not deployed to the live gateway for UI proof in this contribution pass, so live channel screenshots are left for maintainer/requested follow-up. Local runtime/unit coverage covers command registration, set/status/reset, unsupported-channel refusal/no persist, resolver session precedence, and Telegram/Discord/Slack helper precedence.
- Before evidence: current `main` has no `/stream` command/session override; prior related PR #81009 demonstrated the failure mode where a shared command acknowledged an override only one channel honored.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts src/auto-reply/commands-registry.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session-usage.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/preview-streaming.test.ts extensions/discord/src/preview-streaming.test.ts extensions/slack/src/stream-mode.test.ts` — passed, 5 shards / 88 tests.
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`.
- `pnpm check:test-types`.
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` and `git diff --check` — passed.
- Earlier `node scripts/check.mjs --timed` on the first branch head was blocked by stale `npm-shrinkwrap.json` guard before later gates; this PR does not change dependencies.
- Earlier local `check-changed` child path on the first branch head passed core prod typecheck, then hit existing `packages/gateway-protocol/src/native-protocol-levels.guard.test.ts` type errors. `pnpm check:test-types` now passes locally on the current base.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

Highest-risk area: shared command exposure vs channel runtime honoring, the same class of issue that closed #81009.

Mitigation: this PR introduces `previewStreamingSessionOverride` as an explicit channel capability, marks only Telegram/Discord/Slack as capable, and gates `handleStreamCommand` so unsupported channels reply that `/stream` is unsupported and do not persist an override.

## Current review state

- Author implementation complete and pushed as one commit.
- Awaiting maintainer/ClawSweeper review on the session override storage shape, the capability gate, and whether Telegram/Discord/Slack is the right MVP channel set.
- Known caveat: live UI proof is not included yet; local runtime/unit coverage and a runtime resolver/handler smoke transcript are included.

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: Doctor: expose gateway runtime findings
target.body:
## Summary

- adds opt-in structured core Doctor checks for `core/doctor/gateway-health` and `core/doctor/gateway-daemon`
- maps gateway reachability and local daemon service state into `HealthFinding` records
- redacts sensitive Gateway URL material before emitting the gateway health finding `target`
- keeps real Gateway service install/start/restart behavior in the existing legacy Doctor run path

## Behavior

- default `doctor --lint` does not run these checks because both are `defaultEnabled: false`
- explicit `--only core/doctor/gateway-health`, `--only core/doctor/gateway-daemon`, or `--all` can surface the findings
- no config, plugin, or SDK public contract changes

## Validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=verbose` (99 passed)
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`

## Real behavior proof

Local source CLI, Windows worktree, no local Gateway service running:

- `node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/gateway-daemon`
  - exit code: 1
  - result: `ok:false`, `checksRun:1`, one `core/doctor/gateway-daemon` warning: `Gateway service is not installed.`
- `node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/gateway-health`
  - exit code: 1
  - result: `ok:false`, `checksRun:1`, one `core/doctor/gateway-health` warning: `Gateway is not reachable: connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:18789`

Focused regression proof on current head covers a remote Gateway URL containing userinfo and a sensitive query token. The structured finding target is redacted as `wss://***:***@gateway.example.test/rpc?token=***&safe=value`, and the serialized findings do not contain the original userinfo or token value.

Attempted default `node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json`, but the local source command timed out in this fresh worktree. The new checks are default-disabled and covered by focused lint-flow tests plus explicit `--only` CLI proof above.


## Follow-up Fix
- Head `981ba1906fa4ae27d9e20c3c99646fd9ebbf02b2` also points remote Gateway health findings at `gateway.remote.url` instead of the nonexistent `gateway.url`, covering both prepare-failure and unreachable findings.

target.title: Doctor: expose configured plugin install findings
target.body:
## Summary
- add structured `core/doctor/configured-plugin-installs` findings for configured plugins that are missing install records, missing package payloads, deferred during package update, stale version-bound runtime packages, or repairable package metadata
- expose dry-run package repair effects for those findings
- keep real package install/update mutation in the existing legacy configured plugin install repair path
- register the check as lint-only and default-disabled so it only runs when explicitly selected

## Contract
- No public SDK, plugin, or config contract changes.
- No default Doctor behavior change: `core/doctor/configured-plugin-installs` has `defaultEnabled: false` and is reached through explicit lint selection, e.g. `doctor --lint --only core/doctor/configured-plugin-installs`.
- This is split from #96169 so plugin registry findings and configured package install findings can be reviewed independently.

## Validation
- Head: `312da117138d03c93c7ba4b0d816eb5a53cf3b88`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/doctor/shared/missing-configured-plugin-install.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=verbose` (130 passed)
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt.CMD --check src/commands/doctor/shared/missing-configured-plugin-install.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/missing-configured-plugin-install.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxlint.CMD src/commands/doctor/shared/missing-configured-plugin-install.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/missing-configured-plugin-install.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `node scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs --check`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm --package=typescript --package=@types/node --package=@types/react --package=@types/react-dom --package=@types/web --package=@types/ws exec tsgo -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo --declaration false --singleThreaded --checkers 1`

## Source Proof
- Focused detector coverage asserts the missing install record, deferred package repair, and missing package payload cases without invoking package install/update mutation.
- Contribution coverage asserts the lint check id is registered as `defaultEnabled: false` and does not enter the default Doctor set.
- A post-rebase isolated CLI sanity run of `doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/configured-plugin-installs` completed with `checksRun: 1` / `checksSkipped: 27`; current bundled/default state did not recreate the missing external package scenario, so the branch relies on the focused source tests for the failing cases.

No dry-run preview consumer is included in this slice; real package install/update mutation remains in the existing configured plugin install repair path.
